deckDescription: Architectural patterns for building reliable machine learning systems in production. Each card represents a design decision that shapes how your ML platform behaves at scale.

cards.name: Batch Training Pipeline
cards.description: Trains models on a scheduled cadence (nightly, weekly) using a full dataset snapshot. Examples: Spark MLlib, scikit-learn on Airflow.
cards.bestFor: Stable datasets, daily/weekly refresh cycles, when the cost of continuous training outweighs freshness benefits
cards.notGreatFor: Fraud detection or recommendation engines where data drifts within hours — use a streaming training approach

synergies.reason: Pre-computed features reduce training time and improve freshness
Classical ML batch pipelines (tree models, linear models) run on CPU — GPUs sit idle, burning expensive compute for no speedup

cards.name: Continuous / Online Training
cards.description: Model updates incrementally as new data arrives in real time. Examples: Kafka + Flink ML, River (online learning).
cards.bestFor: Rapidly drifting data, personalisation, fraud detection — models that must react to behaviour within minutes
cards.notGreatFor: Small teams or infrequent model changes — the infrastructure overhead is high

synergies.reason: Stream processor feeds training events with minimal lag
HPO trials need stable data to converge — on a streaming pipeline, the data distribution shifts between trials, wasting compute on searches that never settle

cards.name: Batch Inference Engine
cards.description: Runs predictions on a large dataset periodically and stores results for later lookup. Examples: Spark, Dask, BigQuery ML.
cards.bestFor: Pre-computed recommendations, overnight risk scoring, any use case where predictions can be generated ahead of time
cards.notGreatFor: Real-time user-facing predictions where freshness matters — results may already be stale when served

synergies.reason: Consistent features between training and inference reduce skew
Continuous training updates go stale in batch predictions — freshness is wasted

cards.name: Online Inference Service
cards.description: Low-latency model serving endpoint that returns predictions in real time. Examples: TensorFlow Serving, Triton, FastAPI + model.
cards.bestFor: Real-time recommendations, fraud scoring at checkout, any prediction needed synchronously in a user request
cards.notGreatFor: Bulk predictions over millions of rows — batch inference is far cheaper at that scale

synergies.reason: Versioned model promotion enables zero-downtime model swaps

cards.name: Feature Store
cards.description: Centralised repository for computed features with offline (training) and online (inference) access paths. Examples: Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks.
cards.bestFor: Teams reusing features across models, eliminating training-serving skew, point-in-time correct training data
cards.notGreatFor: Early-stage projects with one model — the setup cost exceeds the benefit until you have multiple models sharing features

cards.name: Analytical Data Warehouse
cards.description: Columnar store optimised for large analytical queries. Examples: BigQuery, Redshift, Snowflake.
cards.bestFor: Training data preparation, offline feature computation, business analytics alongside ML workloads
cards.notGreatFor: Low-latency online feature serving — columnar stores are too slow for sub-10ms lookups

synergies.reason: Warehouse serves as the offline store behind the feature store

cards.name: Experiment Tracker
cards.description: Logs hyperparameters, metrics, and artefacts for every training run. Makes results reproducible and comparable. Examples: MLflow, W&B, Comet.
cards.bestFor: Teams running multiple experiments in parallel, reproducing results, comparing model versions before promotion
cards.notGreatFor: Single-model, single-run projects — the overhead isn't worth it yet

synergies.reason: Tracked experiments feed directly into model promotion workflows

cards.name: Model Registry
cards.description: Versioned store for trained model artefacts with staging/production promotion workflows. Examples: MLflow Model Registry, SageMaker Model Registry.
cards.bestFor: Controlled model deployments, rollback capability, audit trails for regulated industries
cards.notGreatFor: Rapid prototyping — model versioning adds process overhead in early stages

cards.name: Stream Processor
cards.description: Processes event streams in real time for feature computation and data enrichment. Examples: Apache Flink, Kafka Streams, Spark Streaming.
cards.bestFor: Real-time feature computation, event-driven training triggers, low-latency data pipelines feeding online inference
cards.notGreatFor: Batch-only workloads — adds operational complexity with no benefit if data is processed hourly or daily

synergies.reason: Real-time features fed directly to the serving layer

cards.name: Model Monitor
cards.description: Tracks data drift, prediction drift, and model performance degradation in production. Alerts when retraining is needed. Examples: Evidently, WhyLabs, Arize.
cards.bestFor: Production models, detecting concept drift, meeting SLAs on model quality
cards.notGreatFor: Pre-production — monitoring adds value only once real traffic is flowing

synergies.reason: Drift alerts can automatically trigger retraining experiments

cards.name: GPU Cluster
cards.description: Dedicated GPU instances for accelerated model training. Examples: AWS p3/p4, GCP A100s, on-prem H100s.
cards.bestFor: Deep learning, LLMs, computer vision — any model architecture where GPU parallelism cuts training time by 10-100x
cards.notGreatFor: Classical ML (tree models, linear models) — CPUs are cheaper and plenty fast

synergies.reason: GPU-accelerated online learning trains faster on incoming data
Classical ML batch pipelines (tree models, linear models) run on CPU — GPUs sit idle, burning expensive compute for no speedup

cards.name: AutoML / HPO
cards.description: Automated hyperparameter optimisation and neural architecture search. Examples: Optuna, Ray Tune, Google AutoML.
cards.bestFor: Systematically finding better models without manual tuning, maximising model performance on a fixed dataset
cards.notGreatFor: Time-sensitive releases — HPO trials can take hours or days; it's an optimisation step, not a shortcut

synergies.reason: Every HPO trial is tracked, making it easy to compare and reproduce the best run
HPO trials need stable data to converge — on a streaming pipeline, the data distribution shifts between trials, wasting compute on searches that never settle
